subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Analysis of Functions
stimulus: The function $f$ is differentiable on the closed interval $[-6, 5]$ and satisfies $f(-2) = 7$. The graph of $f'$, the derivative of $f$, consists of a semicircle and three line segments, as shown in the figure above.

scoring_points.description: Uses the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus: $f(-6) = f(-2) - \int_{-6}^{-2} f'(x)\,dx$
Correct value $f(-6) = 7 - 2\pi$ using the area of the semicircle of radius 2 over $[-6, -2]$ being $\frac{1}{2}\pi(2)^2 = 2\pi$ (above $x$-axis, so $\int_{-6}^{-2} f' = 2\pi$)
Correct value $f(5)$ using $f(5) = f(-2) + \int_{-2}^{5} f'(x)\,dx$ and reading net area from graph (triangles and line segments give net area of $-3$)

parts.prompt: On what intervals is $f$ increasing? Justify your answer.

scoring_points.description: Correct intervals: $f$ is increasing on $(-6, -2)$ where $f' > 0$ (semicircle above $x$-axis), and on $(1, 4)$ where the line segment portion of $f'$ is positive
scoring_points.correct_example: $f$ is increasing on $(-6, -2)$ and $(1, 4)$ because $f'(x) > 0$ on those intervals.

parts.prompt: Find the absolute minimum value of $f$ on the closed interval $[-6, 5]$. Justify your answer.

scoring_points.description: Identifies all critical points and endpoints as candidates, and evaluates $f$ at each: $x = -6$, $x = -2$, $x = 1$, $x = 4$, $x = 5$

required_elements: Considers $f$ at critical points where $f'$ changes sign and at endpoints

scoring_points.description: Correct absolute minimum value with justification

required_elements: Absolute minimum is $7 - 2\pi$ at $x = -6$, or correctly identifies minimum after comparing all candidate values

scoring_points.correct_example: Comparing $f(-6) = 7 - 2\pi \approx 0.72$, $f(-2) = 7$, $f(1)$ (found by subtracting area from $-2$ to $1$), $f(4)$, $f(5) = 4$: the absolute minimum is $7 - 2\pi$ at $x = -6$.

parts.prompt: For each of $f''(-5)$ and $f''(3)$, find the value or explain why it does not exist.

scoring_points.description: $f''(-5)$ exists and equals the slope of the line tangent to the semicircle at $x = -5$ (i.e., $\frac{d}{dx}f'(-5)$, the derivative of $f'$ at $x = -5$)

required_elements: Correct value from the semicircle's derivative at $x = -5$: $f''(-5) = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$ or equivalent from circle geometry

scoring_points.correct_example: The graph of $f'$ is a semicircle of radius 2 centered at $(-4, 0)$. The slope of $f'$ at $x = -5$ equals $\frac{d}{dx}\sqrt{4-(x+4)^2}\big|_{x=-5} = \frac{-(-5+4)}{\sqrt{4-1}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}$.

scoring_points.description: $f''(3)$ does not exist because $f'$ is not differentiable at $x = 3$ — the graph of $f'$ has a corner (the slope of $f'$ changes abruptly) at $x = 3$

required_elements: $f'$ has a corner at $x = 3$, so $f''(3)$ does not exist

scoring_points.correct_example: $f''(3)$ does not exist because the graph of $f'$ has a corner at $x = 3$; the left-hand and right-hand derivatives of $f'$ at $x = 3$ are not equal.
